why_correct: The Anthropic API treats system=None as an explicitly passed null value, which fails schema validation — the parameter must be either a valid string or absent entirely. The correct fix is conditional parameter inclusion: build the kwargs dict and only add 'system' when you have a non-None value to pass. This is the standard Python pattern for optional API parameters and respects the difference between 'omit this field' and 'send this field as null.'

distractors.A: Empty string is tempting because it feels semantically equivalent to 'no system prompt,' but the API may still reject it or interpret it as a malformed instruction block. An empty string is not the same as field absence — you're still sending a system turn, just with no content. A would be correct if the API explicitly documented empty string as the canonical no-op value; it doesn't.
distractors.C: This is the 'retry as error handling' anti-pattern — it looks right until you realize validation errors are deterministic, not transient. Wrapping a 400-class schema violation in retry logic burns throughput, adds latency, and never resolves. Retry logic is appropriate for 529s, 529-equivalent overload responses, or network flakes — not for a payload that will fail identically on every attempt.
distractors.D: Adding a generic fallback system prompt changes the agent's behavior under the assumption that triage calls need no persona — which is the design constraint we're working within. You'd be solving an infra bug by altering product behavior, and now your 'generic' triage handler has prompt real estate to manage, version, and audit.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for optional API parameters: absent ≠ null. Whenever a parameter is optional, your wrapper should build the payload conditionally, not pass None as a sentinel. Any time you see system=None in a shared wrapper, that's a latent validation bug waiting for the code path that actually hits it.
cross_domain_note: This same conditional-inclusion pattern matters in tool_use orchestration — passing an empty tools array vs. omitting tools entirely can change model behavior around tool-calling mode, so the 'only include when meaningful' discipline carries directly into agentic loop construction.

why_correct: The Anthropic Messages API treats system=None as an explicitly passed invalid value, not as an absent parameter — the API schema distinguishes between a field being omitted and a field being present with a null value. The correct pattern is conditional kwargs construction: only inject the 'system' key into the call when a non-None value exists. This is standard wrapper hygiene for any API with strict schema validation on optional fields.

distractors.A: Empty string fails for the same reason None does — the API validates that system, when present, is a non-empty string. Changing the sentinel from None to '' swaps one invalid value for another. A would be correct only if the API accepted empty string as 'no system prompt,' which it does not.
distractors.C: Retry-on-failure is the right pattern for transient network errors or rate limits, not for schema validation errors. Validation errors are deterministic — the retry will fail identically, and you've now doubled latency and burned retry budget on a bug that should be a build-time fix. This is the error-handling-as-architecture anti-pattern.
distractors.D: Whitespace strings still fail schema validation — the API isn't checking for semantic emptiness, it's checking structural validity. Even if it passed, injecting invisible whitespace into the system turn would be a latent correctness bug waiting to surface when behavior is audited.

mental_model: When wrapping any API that has optional fields with strict type validation, never pass a sentinel value for absent optionals — conditionally build the argument dict instead. The rule is: if you wouldn't want the parameter in the raw HTTP body, don't put it in the Python call.
cross_domain_note: This same conditional-kwargs pattern is critical in agentic tool-use wrappers where optional tool_choice or stop_sequences parameters must be omitted entirely rather than nulled out — same API validation surface, same failure mode.

why_correct: Parameterizing the system prompt at the function boundary is the canonical dependency injection pattern for orchestration layers — behavior varies by caller, not by code path. With B, the chat function becomes a pure, reusable primitive; persona logic lives in config or a prompt registry, not in control flow. This scales linearly: adding the three new categories means authoring three new system prompt strings, not touching function signatures or cloning code. The architectural principle is that behavioral variance should be data, not structure.

distractors.A: The failure mode here is token budget waste and prompt brittleness at scale. Consolidating six-plus personas into one conditional system prompt bloats the context window on every call, and intent detection inside the prompt is an unreliable trust boundary — a user turn that looks like a billing inquiry can bleed into technical persona instructions. A is the right pattern only when personas share substantial overlap and the category count is small and stable; neither condition holds here.
distractors.C: This is the clone proliferation anti-pattern — it looks like 'clear separation' until you need to update retry logic, add a streaming flag, or change a shared safety guardrail, at which point you're making the same edit N times. Persona contamination is not a function-boundary problem; it's a system prompt authoring problem. Separate functions don't prevent a badly written system prompt from mixing tones; they just make maintenance O(N).
distractors.D: Database retrieval inside a hard-coded function just relocates the coupling without removing it — the function still owns the lookup logic, so you still can't reuse the function across different retrieval strategies or test it in isolation. This solves the wrong problem.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for this class of problem: if the only thing that changes between call sites is the system prompt, that's a parameter, not a code branch. Any time you see cloned functions or hard-coded strings diverging by category, the architectural tell is that behavior has been promoted to structure — refactor it back to data.
cross_domain_note: This directly impacts prompt caching efficiency: a parameterized system prompt lets you cache at the prompt prefix boundary per persona, whereas a monolithic conditional prompt invalidates the cache across categories and inflates cost per token on high-volume support traffic.

why_correct: The root cause isn't missing exceptions — it's that a prohibition-only system prompt has no positive model of what the agent is supposed to do, so Claude defaults to refusal whenever the action isn't explicitly permitted. Replacing negatives with a role definition and affirmative behavioral rules (what to say at receipt, triage, review stages) gives Claude a positive decision space to operate within, and then prohibitions become surgical: only the actions that carry real legal or business liability get blocked. This is the 'define the lane, not just the guardrails' pattern — it scales because you're specifying intent, not enumerating every edge case.

distractors.A: Appending carve-outs to a prohibition list is additive patching on a fundamentally broken architecture — you're still in negative-space reasoning, just with longer lists. The failure mode is combinatorial: every new edge case requires another explicit exception, and the prompt becomes a fragile catalog of what's allowed rather than a model of the agent's purpose. A is the right fix if the core behavioral model is sound and you need to whitelist one specific action; here, the core model is missing entirely.
distractors.C: Moving constraints to per-turn user instructions conflates runtime flexibility with system-level trust boundaries. Support managers should not have write access to behavioral constraints that carry legal risk — that's a trust boundary violation and a prompt injection surface waiting to happen. Centralized system-prompt governance exists precisely so that policy-sensitive rules aren't editable at the user-turn layer. This 'flexibility' argument sounds like good DevOps but it's actually a security and governance regression.
distractors.D: Routing all ambiguous cases to a human agent doesn't fix the refusal rate — it converts silent refusals into explicit escalations, which is the exact symptom the managers are reporting. You've renamed the failure, not resolved it.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic: prohibition-only prompts create a closed world assumption where everything not explicitly permitted is refused. The correct pattern is positive behavioral specification first, targeted prohibitions second — define what success looks like at each interaction stage, then add hard stops only for genuine risk vectors. If your prompt has more 'do nots' than 'dos', that's the architectural tell.
cross_domain_note: This same positive-vs-negative framing applies in agentic tool-use design: specifying when and how a tool should be called (positive) produces more reliable behavior than listing prohibited tool invocations (negative), because the model has a goal to pursue rather than a minefield to navigate.

why_correct: The rule 'request clarification instead' is an unbounded trigger — it fires on any non-zero uncertainty rather than only on inputs that genuinely exceed a classification threshold. Without a specificity condition, Claude optimizes for the stated constraint (avoid misclassification) at the expense of the unstated one (minimize clarification rate). Adding 'only when the field cannot be inferred from surrounding context' transforms an open-ended escape hatch into a bounded fallback, which aligns the model's uncertainty tolerance with what a human reviewer would accept.

distractors.A: Persona-rule conflict is a real failure mode, but it produces inconsistent behavior — flip-flopping between extraction and refusal — not a systematic 31% clarification rate. The architectural tell here is that 94% extraction accuracy is high and stable; if persona conflict were the root cause, you'd see noisier outputs. Persona removal doesn't change the trigger condition for clarification; it just removes a framing cue that's actually doing useful work.
distractors.C: Few-shot examples would help calibrate uncertainty, but they address the symptom through the training-data pathway rather than the constraint pathway. The correct pattern when a behavioral rule is underspecified is to fix the rule first — few-shot examples can't override an explicit instruction, and in production, prompt injection or edge cases can push the model back toward the unqualified rule. C is a reasonable second step after B, not a substitute for it.
distractors.D: Reducing the extraction schema changes what fields are extracted, not how the model handles uncertainty within any given field. In production, a narrower schema with the same underspecified clarification rule will just produce the same clarification rate against fewer fields — the trigger mechanism is unchanged.

mental_model: When a behavioral rule produces a systematic threshold problem — too aggressive, too conservative — the fix is almost always to add a specificity condition to the rule itself, not to restructure the surrounding prompt architecture. The CCA heuristic: if a fallback instruction lacks 'when' and 'only when' qualifiers, it will fire greedily on any uncertainty.
cross_domain_note: This is the same constraint-scoping principle that governs tool-use instructions in agentic loops — an unbounded 'call this tool when unsure' directive will produce excessive tool calls just as an unbounded clarification rule produces excessive clarification requests. Always gate fallback behaviors with explicit conditions.

why_correct: max_tokens is a hard ceiling enforced by the inference layer — the model doesn't negotiate or round up to the nearest sentence boundary, it stops generating at exactly token N and returns whatever exists at that cutoff. The architectural failure here is conflating two call types with fundamentally different output-length contracts inside a single shared parameter configuration. Health-check pings have a trivially small output contract (a status token or two), while multi-step troubleshooting flows require enough token budget to complete a coherent reasoning chain. A global ceiling sized for one call type silently corrupts the other at runtime, producing no error signal — just truncated instructions reaching live customers.

distractors.A: Claude doesn't throw on truncation — it returns a completed (but cut-off) response with a stop_reason of 'max_tokens' rather than 'end_turn'. The health-check loop wouldn't break; it would silently succeed with partial output. A would be directionally relevant if the API contract threw a 4xx on ceiling breach, which it doesn't.
distractors.C: This is the attention-budget misconception — the model doesn't perform lookahead over the token ceiling to reallocate attention. max_tokens is an inference stop condition, not a parameter Claude 'sees' during forward passes. The quality degradation in this scenario is purely structural truncation, not any change in how the model weights earlier tokens.
distractors.D: Separating the client instances is a reasonable operational hygiene point, but it doesn't address the root problem: if the team separates clients but still sets max_tokens=50 on the customer-facing wrapper, the truncation bug persists. The architectural tell is that the question asks why to reject the global ceiling proposal specifically — D sidesteps that entirely and treats a deployment topology fix as a substitute for the correct parameter scoping fix.

mental_model: The CCA heuristic for max_tokens is: always scope it to the output contract of the call type, never to cost targets globally. When multiple call types share infrastructure, the parameter set must be parameterized per call type — a shared global ceiling will always be wrong for at least one of them. Truncation produces no exception, so the failure is silent until a customer reports it.
cross_domain_note: This is the same class of problem as prompt caching key design — when you have heterogeneous call types sharing infrastructure, any parameter or cache key scoped globally rather than per-call-type will either over-constrain one type or under-constrain another. Cost optimization decisions need to be made at the call-type boundary, not the wrapper boundary.

why_correct: max_tokens is a per-call parameter, not a system-wide constant — treating it as a global setting is the architectural mistake here. The correct pattern is to scope resource constraints to the call's purpose: connectivity checks need only a short confirmation token (≤50), so capping them tightly prevents the model from burning budget on unprompted verbosity, while extraction calls get the higher ceiling they need to emit complete structured output. This decoupling solves both problems simultaneously — no truncation on extraction, no token waste on smoke tests — without touching the cost baseline.

distractors.A: Prompt-level conciseness instructions are probabilistic, not deterministic — you're asking the model to self-regulate token output, which it won't do reliably under load or across model versions. The failure mode is inconsistent compliance: conciseness instructions reduce average verbosity but don't enforce a hard ceiling, so you still risk smoke-test calls consuming 600–800 tokens and extraction calls hitting the same 1000-token wall. A is the right tool when you want stylistic brevity in responses; it's the wrong tool when you need a hard resource constraint.
distractors.B: Raising the global ceiling to 4096 fixes truncation but directly contradicts the stated constraint of not increasing overall token spend. In production, this also widens the blast radius on any runaway agentic call or prompt injection that triggers a verbose response. The near-miss quality here is that it correctly identifies max_tokens as the lever — it just applies it in the wrong direction and at the wrong scope.
distractors.D: Removing the system prompt from smoke-test calls changes response style unpredictably and doesn't enforce any token ceiling — Claude without a system prompt can still return a lengthy default response. More critically, keeping max_tokens=1000 globally leaves the extraction truncation problem completely unresolved. This is the 'remove configuration hoping behavior changes' anti-pattern; it looks like a fix until the first verbose default response blows past what you expected.

mental_model: Whenever you have two call types with fundamentally different output size requirements sharing one configuration, parameterize the constraint — don't try to tune a single global value to satisfy both. The CCA heuristic: if a parameter varies by use case, it belongs in the call site, not the shared config.
cross_domain_note: This same per-call parameterization principle applies to cost optimization and latency SLA design — tiering model selection (Haiku vs. Sonnet) per call type follows identical logic to tiering max_tokens, and CCA questions on cost architecture often test whether you recognize that shared configs are a smell when call types diverge.

why_correct: The None default is a configuration gap — the function has no guaranteed behavioral contract, so invocation without a system prompt produces undefined output format. Prepending format instructions to every user message patches the symptom per-call instead of fixing the architectural defect: that the extraction role and output schema are not established at the system level where they belong. The correct fix binds behavioral guidance to the call itself via a system prompt, so format consistency is enforced regardless of which code path invokes the function. This is the 'configuration as call contract' principle — pipeline-critical constraints belong in the layer that governs behavior, not scattered across message payloads.

distractors.A: System prompt and user message instructions are not functionally equivalent for output structure. System prompts establish persistent role and behavioral constraints before the conversation begins; user-turn instructions are contextually scoped and more easily overridden or diluted by complex message content. The failure mode in production: as ticket text gets longer or more ambiguous, the inline format instruction loses weight relative to the content, and prose drift returns.
distractors.C: The near-miss here is that response_format/JSON mode is a real mechanism — but it's the wrong layer for this fix. JSON mode enforces syntactic validity of the output, not semantic correctness of the extraction schema or role. More importantly, the stem's root cause is a missing system prompt establishing extraction behavior, not a missing API-level format constraint. Reaching for response_format without fixing the configuration gap just adds a second patch on top of the first defect.
distractors.D: The claim that prompt caching causes system prompts to not apply to subsequent turns is a misunderstanding of how caching works. Cache hits serve the same prompt content — caching is a latency and cost optimization, not a behavioral bypass. This distractor collapses the distinction between cache mechanics and prompt application semantics.

mental_model: When a pipeline has a behavioral consistency failure, ask which layer owns that behavior — if it's output format or role, it belongs in the system prompt, full stop. The CCA heuristic: per-message patching of a configuration gap is always wrong, because it moves a single-point fix into N invocation sites and breaks the moment any caller omits it.
cross_domain_note: This connects directly to the agentic/orchestration domain: in multi-agent pipelines, the system prompt is the trust boundary where role and behavioral constraints are locked in. Letting that layer default to None is equivalent to running a tool-use agent with no instructions — downstream parsing failures are the benign version of what can go wrong.
